1: The antinomian objection — "shall we go on sinning so that grace may increase?" — was not merely hypothetical. Paul's teaching that "where sin increased, grace increased all the more" (5:20) was exactly the kind of statement that could be misread as moral license, and apparently was (cf. the polemic against those who say "let us do evil that good may come," 3:8). The objection follows the internal logic of Paul's argument, which makes it a serious challenge rather than a straw man. Paul's answer in ch6 is not "no, because you will be punished" but "no, because you have died" — a fundamentally different kind of answer.
2: The "by no means!" (mē genoito) is Paul's strongest negative, used thirteen times in the letter. The rhetorical form is that of a diatribe — a teaching style common in philosophical schools where an imaginary interlocutor poses objections that the teacher demolishes. Traces of this form can be found in Epictetus, Dio Chrysostom, and other popular philosophers of the period; Paul has adapted it for theological instruction. The congregation at Rome would have been familiar with the diatribe form from popular philosophy.
3: The appeal to baptism as a shared practice ("don't you know that all of us who were baptized...") assumes that baptism was universally received in the Roman congregation and that its meaning was understood. In Jewish practice, proselyte baptism (immersion in a mikveh) was the ritual that marked the transition from Gentile to Jew — a death-to-old-identity and birth-to-new-identity. John's baptism adapted this with an explicitly repentance-oriented meaning. Christian baptism, as Paul describes it, carries the same structural symbolism but now explicitly connected to Christ's own death and burial.
4: Burial with Christ "through baptism into death" unpacks the full symbolic weight of immersion: going under the water is going into the tomb. The image of being buried was more than metaphor in the ancient world — burial was the final seal of death, the act that confirmed no return. Roman burial practices were elaborate and significant: both cremation and inhumation marked the transition from this world to the next. Paul's point is that baptism is the ritual equivalent of burial — it confirms and seals the death that participation in Christ's death involves. The new life that follows is resurrection life, not merely moral reform.
5: The Greek symphytoi (grown-together-with, united with) is an agricultural term: two plants grafted together such that they share a common rootstock and cannot be separated without destroying both. Paul uses the term to express an organic union with Christ rather than a merely moral imitation of him. This is distinct from the Stoic ideal of "following the sage" — the Christian is not modeling herself on Christ from the outside but has been united with him in a way that makes his history her history. The resurrection is as certain as the death: the graft that takes on one side takes on the other.
6: The "old self" (palaios anthrōpos, old human) belongs to Paul's two-age framework: the old age (this present evil age, under sin's dominion) and the new age (inaugurated in Christ's resurrection). The "old self" is not simply the pre-conversion individual but the solidarity-identity that the person shared with Adam — the Adamic humanity that belongs to the old age. Its crucifixion with Christ is the termination of that solidarity: the believer's "body of sin" (the fleshly nature as the organ through which sin operates) is rendered inoperative (katargeō — nullified, put out of service).
7: The maxim "anyone who has died has been set free from sin" (dedikaiōtai apo tēs hamartias) uses the same verb as "justified" throughout chs3-5, here in a new sense: death itself frees from the master's claim. This reflects a genuine legal principle in Roman law: death dissolved legal obligations. A slave's death dissolved the bond to the master; a debtor's death ended the debt obligation. Paul uses the legal principle to describe the spiritual reality: union with Christ's death ends the juridical claim that sin had over the believer.
8: The confidence of "we believe that we will also live with him" draws on Jewish resurrection hope, but now anchored in the specific event of Christ's resurrection as the prototype. Early Jewish texts (Dan 12:2; 2 Macc 7:9; 4 Macc; 1 Enoch) spoke of resurrection for the righteous at the end of the age. Paul's claim is that this general resurrection hope is now particularized: Christ's resurrection is the first-fruits (1 Cor 15:23), and those united with him will share in the harvest. The general becomes specific; the future becomes proleptically available now.
9: The statement that "death no longer has mastery over him" addresses a question the resurrection narrative itself raises: could a risen Christ die again? The language of "mastery" (kyrieuō, to lord over, have dominion) is the language of authority and rule. Death exercised its lordship over Christ once; the resurrection ended that lordship permanently. This is not merely a claim about Christ's individual biography but about the nature of death itself: in the new age, death has been decisively defeated and can no longer exercise its authority over the risen Lord — or, through him, over those who belong to him.
10: The phrase "died to sin once for all" (ephapax) was theologically loaded: ephapax means "once, once-for-all, never to be repeated." This word appears in Hebrews as the key term for the unrepeatable nature of Christ's sacrifice over against the repeated animal sacrifices of the temple system (Heb 7:27; 9:12; 10:10). Paul's use here underlines the finality of Christ's death with respect to sin: it was not a general sacrifice needing repetition but a singular, definitive act. The parallel with the Day of Atonement (the once-yearly sacrifice for all Israel) would have been intelligible to Jewish readers.
11: The command to "count yourselves dead to sin" (logizesthe, reckon, consider) uses the same accounting verb that dominated ch4 (credited, reckoned). There the verb described God's act of crediting faith as righteousness; here it describes the believer's act of appropriating the death-to-sin reality as her own. The point is not that the believer creates a new reality by thinking about it, but that she is called to recognize and inhabit the reality that God has established: you are dead to sin's authority — live accordingly. The indicative grounds the imperative.
12: The command "do not let sin reign in your mortal body" takes up the royal language of 5:21 ("sin reigned in death") and turns it into a moral imperative. In the Roman world, the concept of a slave revolting against an unjust claim of ownership was comprehensible — if a person had been legally freed but a former master continued to demand service, the freed person was under no obligation to comply. Paul's point: sin's legal claim on the believer has been terminated by Christ's death; the believer is therefore under no obligation to obey sin's commands, even when the passions of the mortal body still clamor for compliance.
13: The call to offer the body's members as "instruments of righteousness" (hopla dikaiosynēs) — the word hopla is also "weapons," used for military equipment — introduces the warfare metaphor that Paul will develop in Eph 6 and 2 Cor 10:4. The believer's bodily members are presented as contested territory: sin presents a claim to use them as weapons of wickedness; Paul commands their dedication to God as weapons of righteousness. The imagery of holy war, drawn from the OT tradition of the ḥerem (dedication of conquered territory to God), underlies the call to total bodily consecration.
14: The claim that believers are "not under law but under grace" would have been immediately provocative to Jewish members of the congregation. Paul is not saying the Torah is evil or irrelevant, but that it is not the governing framework for the believer's relationship to God. The law operates in one sphere (defining standards, exposing transgression, pronouncing judgment); grace operates in another (liberating from condemnation, enabling new life). Being transferred from law's sphere to grace's sphere does not mean the believer may sin, as the following verse makes clear, but that the mechanism of the relationship to God has fundamentally changed.
15: The antinomian objection is raised a second time, now in a slightly different form: "shall we sin because we are not under law but under grace?" The repetition shows Paul is aware this is the most dangerous misreading of his position. The twice-posed objection and twice-given "by no means!" creates a rhetorical frame around the whole ch6 argument, bookending it. This is deliberate structure: Paul knew that his doctrine of grace was vulnerable to the libertine misreading, and he returns to it precisely because the accusation was apparently in circulation.
16: The master-slave analogy was not an abstraction in first-century Rome — it was a daily social reality. The city's population included a substantial enslaved class (estimates vary: perhaps 30–40% of Rome's inhabitants were enslaved or freedpersons). The congregation at Rome itself likely included enslaved persons, slave-owners, and freedpersons. Paul's use of slavery as the primary metaphor for sin's dominion was not distant; it was visceral. The phrase "slaves of whomever you obey" reflects the Roman legal definition: the slave is the property of the master and must obey. But here the obedience determines the master, not the other way round.
17: The "pattern of teaching" (typos didachēs) to which the Roman believers "obeyed from the heart" suggests an established catechetical tradition — a summary of Christian belief and ethics that new converts were formed in. This is early evidence of creedal formation: the Roman church had a recognizable doctrinal shape that shaped its members. The phrase "claimed your allegiance" (literally: "to which you were delivered") reflects the idea that the believer is herself the thing handed over — she has been placed in the care of the teaching, as a deposit entrusted to a guardian.
18: Being "set free from sin and becoming slaves of righteousness" captures the paradox of Christian freedom: freedom from sin's dominion is not absolute independence but a change of masters. In Roman manumission law, the freed slave became a libertinus (freedman/freedwoman) but typically remained a client of the former master — bound by obligations of loyalty and service, even if no longer legally owned. Paul's terminology is harder than manumission: not clienthood but a new slavery, to righteousness and to God. The counter-intuitive point is that freedom-toward-God is true freedom, while freedom-from-God is bondage.
19: The explicit acknowledgment — "I am using an example from everyday life because of your human limitations" — is Paul's own commentary on the slavery metaphor. He knows the analogy is imperfect (being enslaved to God is nothing like being enslaved to a human master) but uses it because its social reality was immediately intelligible to his readers. The limitation of the analogy lies in its being drawn from a domain of coercion; the reality it points to is a loving, voluntary, transformative loyalty. The ancient audience's familiarity with the economics of slavery makes the contrast between the two "masterships" all the more stark.
20: The phrase "free from the control of righteousness" describes the condition of slavery to sin: from sin's perspective, righteousness had no claim on you. This is the inverse of the post-conversion condition ("free from sin, enslaved to God"). The two mutually exclusive lordships — sin and God — cannot both hold sway simultaneously. The image reflects the social reality: a slave owned by one master cannot simultaneously be owned by another, nor is he bound by the obligations owed to a different household. The transfer of lordship is complete and exclusive.
21: The question "what benefit did you reap at that time?" addresses the Roman congregation's past — many of them would have come from backgrounds involving the sexual practices, idolatry, and social vices that Paul elsewhere calls the "things you are now ashamed of." The shame (epaischynomai) they now feel is itself the evidence of transformation: the moral judgment that condemns the past practices is a new capacity, the result of the Spirit's presence. Their past practices now appear to them as they really were: barren and death-producing.
22: The contrast between the former "slavery to sin" (producing death) and the present "slavery to God" (producing holiness and eternal life) maps onto the two ages: the old age under sin's dominion, and the new age inaugurated in Christ. The term "holiness" (hagiasmos, sanctification, being-set-apart) was the covenantal goal of Israel's election (Lev 11:44: "be holy, for I am holy"). Paul claims that what Israel was called to through Torah-keeping is now being realized in Gentile believers through the Spirit — the boundary between Israel's holiness and Gentile uncleanness has been redrawn through Christ.
23: The military term opsōnion (wages, army pay — literally the "cooked rations" allowance) appears here: sin's wages. Roman soldiers were paid their opsōnion in coin or kind at regular intervals; it was their contracted due. The irony is that sin pays its servants exactly what they have earned — death. Over against this, the "gift of God" (charisma tou theou) is explicitly not wages but a free bestowal. The contrast between what is earned and what is given without merit has been building since ch4's wage analogy (4:4-5). Romans 6:23 is the aphoristic summary of the entire argument.
